---
title: "A single short MCMC chain cannot carry the HRM"
---

The concern in this chapter is narrow: it is about how *long* the MCMC sampler ran, and
what that means for trusting the specific numerical comparisons drawn from it. This
discussion appears on pages 60–61.

## What the dissertation does

The HRM is estimated with the R package `immer`, which — as the dissertation notes — "provides one
chain" (p. 60). That single chain runs 1,000 iterations, of which the first 300 are
discarded as *burn-in* (the warm-up phase whose draws are thrown away while the sampler
is still finding its way around the posterior). The remaining ~700 draws are the entire
posterior sample — the pool from which every estimate, interval, and between-condition
comparison is read.

For a sense of scale, the benchmarks cited on the same page: `immer`'s own default is
500,000 iterations with 200,000 burn-in; Patz et al. (2002) used 10,000 / 1,000;
Casabianca et al. used 105,000 / 5,000 (p. 60). The chain is between one-fifteenth and
one-hundredth as long as established practice. The priors are diffuse:
$\phi_r \sim N(0, 10^2)$, $\psi_r \sim N(0.4, 10^2)$, and
$\sigma^2 \sim \text{InverseGamma}(0.001, 1)$ (p. 61).

To check convergence, the dissertation reports a Gelman–Rubin $\hat{R}$ — a diagnostic that compares
*between-chain* to *within-chain* variance, with values near 1.0 signaling that chains
started from different places have settled on the same distribution. But a real $\hat{R}$
needs at least two chains, and only one was run; so the reported value is computed "by
splitting a single chain into three segments and treat[ing] them as independent chains"
(p. 61). Even that weaker substitute flags trouble: "some parameters, such as $a_i$,
exhibited high Rhat values, indicating potential convergence issues" (p. 61). The
dissertation concludes the focal results were not strongly affected — but that claim is hard to
verify with the evidence at hand.

## Why this threatens the conclusion

The core issue is that 700 draws from one chain is *not* 700 independent draws. A Markov
chain produces **autocorrelated** samples — each draw resembles the one before it — so it
must run long enough for that correlation to decay before it behaves like a random
sample. The **effective sample size** corrects for this: if the integrated
autocorrelation time is $\tau$, then $\text{ESS} \approx 700/\tau$. For a hierarchical
IRT model with hundreds of correlated parameters (an ability for every examinee, an item
vector for every item, and every $\phi_r$ and $\psi_r$), estimated by the single-site
Metropolis–Hastings sampler `immer` uses, $\tau$ is commonly in the tens to hundreds. At
$\tau = 20, 50, 100$, those ~700 retained draws give an ESS of only **35, 14, and 7** — a few dozen
genuinely independent draws at best, dwindling to single digits. The remedy is built into
that same arithmetic: because $\text{ESS} \approx N/\tau$, a chain ten times longer would
lift these to roughly 350, 140, and 70. The problem is not
autocorrelation itself but that 700 draws is too thin a pool to absorb it, and lengthening
the chain is exactly what raises the ESS.

That matters because every reported posterior mean carries a **Monte-Carlo standard
error** — the uncertainty from using a finite, autocorrelated sample rather than the true
posterior: $\text{MCSE} = \text{posterior SD} / \sqrt{\text{ESS}}$. With
$\sigma_\phi \approx 0.5$, the MCSE at those ESS values is about **0.085, 0.134, and
0.189** — the *same size* as the between-condition $\phi$ differences of roughly
0.05–0.16 interpreted as meaningful. In other words, the Monte-Carlo noise in the
estimate is as large as the reported signal. @fig-mcmc-short shows both halves of this.

```{r}
#| label: fig-mcmc-short
#| fig-cap: "A single short chain cannot carry the HRM. **(A)** Two independent chains of the same length (1,000 iterations, 300 burn-in) under single-site-MH-like autocorrelation land at post-burn-in means about 0.14 apart — a gap a *single* chain cannot reveal. **(B)** The Monte-Carlo standard error of φ versus the autocorrelation time τ: across the realistic range it lands inside the very band of between-condition differences the study interprets (0.05–0.16); ESS = 700/τ is 35/14/7 at τ = 20/50/100. *This is an honest AR(1) surrogate calibrated to the regime — not a re-run of immer; the ESS/MCSE relations are exact algebra.*"
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4.2
#| code-summary: "Show the R code (illustrative AR(1) surrogate + exact ESS/MCSE)"
suppressPackageStartupMessages({ library(ggplot2); library(patchwork) })
source("R/common.R")

set.seed(2026)
N_KEEP <- 700L; SD_PHI <- 0.5; BURN <- 300L; LEN <- 1000L
DIFF_LO <- 0.05; DIFF_HI <- 0.16
ess  <- function(tau) N_KEEP / tau
mcse <- function(tau) SD_PHI / sqrt(ess(tau))
tau_seq <- seq(1, 150, length.out = 400)

ar1 <- function(rho, n, sd = SD_PHI, mu = 0) {          # single-site-MH-like autocorrelation
  x <- numeric(n); x[1] <- rnorm(1, mu, sd); inn <- rnorm(n, 0, sd * sqrt(1 - rho^2))
  for (t in 2:n) x[t] <- mu + rho * (x[t - 1] - mu) + inn[t]; x
}
rho_typ <- (50 - 1) / (50 + 1)                          # τ = 50
chA <- ar1(rho_typ, LEN); chB <- ar1(rho_typ, LEN)
mA <- mean(chA[(BURN + 1):LEN]); mB <- mean(chB[(BURN + 1):LEN])

# Panel A — two single chains disagree
dfA <- rbind(data.frame(it = 1:LEN, phi = chA, chain = "Chain A"),
             data.frame(it = 1:LEN, phi = chB, chain = "Chain B"))
pA <- ggplot(dfA, aes(it, phi, colour = chain)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = BURN, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf, fill = "grey85", alpha = .6) +
  annotate("text", x = BURN/2, y = 1.7, label = "burn-in\n(300 discarded)", size = 2.7,
           colour = "grey45", lineheight = .9) +
  geom_line(linewidth = .35, alpha = .9) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mA, colour = pal_companion[["misfit"]], linetype = "dashed", linewidth = .8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mB, colour = pal_companion[["severe"]], linetype = "dashed", linewidth = .8) +
  annotate("label", x = 1000, y = 1.92, hjust = 1, vjust = 1, size = 2.55, colour = "grey25",
           fill = "white", alpha = .85, label.size = 0, lineheight = .9,
           label = sprintf("the two post-burn-in means differ by %.2f\n— a single chain cannot reveal this", abs(mA - mB))) +
  scale_colour_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Chain A" = pal_companion[["misfit"]], "Chain B" = pal_companion[["severe"]])) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 300, 600, 1000)) +
  labs(title = "(A)  Two single 1,000 / 300 chains disagree",
       subtitle = "illustrative AR(1) surrogate — not a re-run of immer", x = "MCMC iteration", y = "φ draw") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(legend.position = "bottom", plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9))

# Panel B — MCSE vs τ, with the interpreted-effect band
dfB <- data.frame(tau = tau_seq, mcse = mcse(tau_seq))
pts <- data.frame(tau = c(20, 50, 100), mcse = mcse(c(20, 50, 100)), ess = ess(c(20, 50, 100)))
pB <- ggplot(dfB, aes(tau, mcse)) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = -Inf, xmax = Inf, ymin = DIFF_LO, ymax = DIFF_HI,
           fill = pal_companion[["accent"]], alpha = .15) +
  annotate("text", x = 148, y = 0.088, hjust = 1, vjust = .5, size = 2.85, lineheight = .9,
           colour = pal_companion[["accent"]], fontface = "italic",
           label = "between-condition φ\ndifferences interpreted\n(0.05–0.16)") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1, colour = "#34495E") +
  geom_point(data = pts, aes(tau, mcse), colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], size = 3) +
  geom_text(data = pts, aes(tau, mcse, label = sprintf("ESS = %.0f", ess)),
            vjust = -1.1, size = 3, colour = pal_companion[["warn"]], fontface = "bold") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1, 20, 50, 100, 150)) + scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.25)) +
  labs(title = "(B)  Monte-Carlo error rivals the 'effects'",
       subtitle = "R-hat undefined with one chain;  ESS = 700 / τ,  MCSE = 0.5 / √ESS",
       x = "integrated autocorrelation time  τ  (swept, not estimated)", y = "MCSE of φ posterior mean") +
  theme_companion(11) + theme(plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9),
                              panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

pA | pB
```

A note on the figure: it is an *illustrative surrogate* calibrated
to the regime of single-site Metropolis–Hastings on a hierarchical posterior — not a
re-run of the `immer` model, since the data are not available. The ESS and MCSE
relationships are exact algebra; the chain-disagreement panel illustrates what is
*possible* in this regime, not a claim about the sampler's exact $\tau$. The arithmetic,
worked from first principles, reaches the same place.

Two further points. First, the high $\hat{R}$ for $a_i$ cannot be safely bracketed: in a
hierarchical model $a_i$, $\theta$, $\xi$, $\phi$, and $\psi$ are coupled in the joint
posterior, so a sampling problem in one is a warning about the whole. The dissertation does not leave
this unexamined: it re-checks with longer chains and finds the focal patterns stable
(p. 60), and it compares the $a_i$-estimated against the $a_i$-fixed specification across
20 replications, where the results were consistent (a moderate, positive correlation, with
the mean $\phi$ and $\psi$ holding up; p. 61). What those checks cannot do is *substitute* for a convergence statistic: a stable
pattern across longer runs, or matching means across replications, is a robustness
observation — it tells a reader the result is reproducible — not a formal $\hat{R}$/ESS
demonstration that the coupled sampler has converged.
Second, the diffuse priors compound the issue: $\text{InverseGamma}(0.001, 1)$ on
$\sigma^2$ is extremely diffuse — its mode sits near 1 and, with shape $0.001 < 1$, its
mean is not even finite, so it spreads enormous prior mass over large $\sigma^2$ (a very
heavy right tail) rather than piling mass near zero. The Limitations section (p. 74) flags that
`immer`'s default inverse-gamma prior "has raised concerns in previous research," citing
Gelman (2006) by name. The one nuance to add is
*which* form Gelman criticized: the pathology is the $\text{InverseGamma}(\epsilon,
\epsilon)$ case — both parameters tiny — whose pile-up of mass near zero is the genuinely
long-criticized behavior, and that is not the $\text{InverseGamma}(0.001, 1)$ used here. With a
scale of 1, this prior behaves the opposite way near the boundary, so Gelman's specific
warning does not land on it. Even so, such a
heavy-tailed prior on $\sigma^2$ can slow mixing, and when convergence is uncertain its
influence on $\phi$ and $\psi$ cannot be cleanly separated from sampling noise.

::: {.callout-note}
## The strongest counterargument

The record shows convergence was not ignored. The high $\hat{R}$ for some parameters is reported openly in the Limitations
(p. 74); additional checks with *longer chains* found the focal patterns stable
(p. 60); the $a_i$-estimated specification was compared against the $a_i$-fixed specification across *20
replications* (p. 61) with consistent results; and the dissertation flags that `immer`'s default
inverse-gamma prior "has raised concerns in previous research," citing Gelman (2006). This
is more convergence diligence than most applied MFRM papers show.

The issue was never *awareness*; it is the *form* of the evidence. "The longer chains looked stable" and
"the means matched across 20 replications" are robustness observations, not convergence
statistics — they tell a reader the result is plausible, not that it is settled. The journal
version needs those same checks *formalized*: a true multi-chain $\hat{R}$, bulk- and
tail-ESS, and an MCSE for each focal $\phi$ and $\psi$, so that "the patterns were stable"
becomes "ESS = X, MCSE = Y, and each interpreted difference exceeds its MCSE by a comfortable
margin." The judgment-laden part is done; what remains is to report it in the
currency the reviewer counts in.
:::

## What would fix it

::: {.callout-tip}
## Fix-it
- **Run ≥ 4 independent chains** from dispersed starting values — the prerequisite for a
  meaningful $\hat{R}$ — each much longer than 1,000/300 (treat Patz's 10,000/1,000 or
  Casabianca's 105,000/5,000 as a floor, and extend if diagnostics warrant). The obstacle
  is that `immer` provides only one chain by default, so there are two realistic routes to
  the four. The immediate, low-cost one: run `immer` ≥ 4 times from different seeds and
  dispersed starting values, then pool the runs as chains and compute the diagnostics
  across them. The more thorough one — and the better long-run investment — is to
  re-implement the HRM in a native multi-chain sampler (Stan, JAGS, or NIMBLE), which runs
  the chains jointly and yields proper multi-chain $\hat{R}$, bulk-/tail-ESS, and MCSE
  directly.
- **Report, for every focal parameter** ($\phi_r$, $\psi_r$), the true multi-chain
  $\hat{R}$, the bulk- and tail-ESS, and the MCSE — and state in the text that each
  interpreted difference exceeds its MCSE by a comfortable margin.
- **Show trace plots and per-chain posterior-density overlays** for the focal parameters
  as supplementary material — the most direct evidence a reader can inspect.
- **Run a prior-sensitivity analysis** for $\phi$ and $\psi$; replace
  $\text{InverseGamma}(0.001,1)$ with a half-Normal or half-Cauchy on the SD scale (which
  mixes far better), and consider a $\log(\psi)$ / positivity-constrained parameterization
  to avoid boundary pathologies.
- **Add posterior-predictive checks** — simulate replicated ratings from the posterior
  and compare to the observed data — as independent evidence of fit and reach.
:::

## What a reviewer will ask

::: {.callout-warning}
## What a reviewer will ask
"What are the effective sample size and Monte-Carlo standard error of $\phi$ and $\psi$ at
700 draws from a single chain — and are the between-condition differences you interpret
larger than that Monte-Carlo error? And how was an $\hat{R}$ computed from a single chain?"
:::

`immer` provides a single chain by default,
and a short run is an understandable choice under real time and compute limits — a
software-and-resources constraint, not a methodological error. The point is only what the
*journal* version needs to show. Under the current setup the ESS
is likely single digits to low tens, the MCSE is of the same order as the reported differences,
and the $\hat{R}$ came from a split-chain approximation that itself flagged problems.
The goal of the revision is
to answer with specific numbers — ESS = X, MCSE = Y, interpreted difference =
Z, and a large Z/Y. This is achievable, and getting there makes the
HRM results persuasive rather than merely plausible.
